data.title: Chart Of The Day: Is It Finally This Chip Stock's Time To Shine?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Intel Corp. (NASDAQ:INTC) has been an also-ran in the semiconductor industry for a long time - and the stock has languished for years. Until now. So... is it finally this chip stock’s time to shine?

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It’s a weekly chart of INTC going back a half-decade. The stock was down more than 31% over that time frame through Wednesday. During the same period, former archrival Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) rose 107%, while the sector’s rockstar Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) soared 1,350%!

INTC (5-Year Chart)

(Data by YCharts)

But late last year, the stock stopped going down. Then, as INTC shares chopped sideways for a few months, trading volume increased dramatically. Plus, the Relative Strength Index marched steadily higher.

Nothing compared to what happened this week though. The stock rocketed as much as 30% Thursday on news that NVDA - of all companies - was linking up with INTC. Specifically, Nvidia said it would buy $5 billion in Intel shares, plus work with the company to co-develop chips for personal computers and data centers.

The news followed the Trump Administration’s decision for the US to take a 10% stake in Intel. Japan’s SoftBank Group Corp. (OTCPK:SFTBY) also recently invested $2 billion of its money in Intel. In other words, both public and private capital is flowing into Intel on a scale we haven’t seen in years.

Joe Markman of Digital Creators & Consumers named Intel one of his “Top Picks” in our 2025 annual report project. He called it a “compelling” opportunity, adding “semiconductors, and their production, are now important components of national security. Too much production is concentrated in Asia. The US needs a strong national player, and that company is necessarily Intel.”

Sure enough, the rest of the market seems to be figuring out the same thing. And yes, it does look like it’s this chip stock’s time to shine!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Moncler: From Neutral To Buy Ahead Of Winter Season
data.description:
We return to comment on Moncler (OTCPK:MONRF) (OTCPK:MONRY) and, looking back at our downgrade in mid-February 2025, it proved timely as the stock has since shed nearly 20% of its market capitalization (Fig. 1). Our neutral stance was supported by a stretched valuation (with more P/E discount) and FX headwinds that were not reflected in the stock price. That said, with the winter season approaching, and after a quick review of Q2 results, we reassess what has shifted in our outlook.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive View

We should report that Q2 is Moncler's smallest quarter in terms of sales. In number, it represents 13% of the company's total yearly sales. The company's Q2 sales reached €397 million, bringing the H1 results to €1.225 billion. This represents an increase of 1% compared to H1 2024 and -1% versus the Q2 2024 results (Fig. 2).

Fig 2

By brand, in Q2, Moncler reported a minus 2%, while the company reported a plus 6% in Stone Island. By region, Asia remains flattish, the Americas saw solid support with a plus 5%, while the EU was down by 8%. Going down to the P&L assessment, H1 gross margin reached 76.9% with a consensus expectation of 77.0%. Moncler EBIT reached €224.8 million in H1 with a core operating profit margin of 18.3%. This represents a minus 270 basis points lower than H1 2024, mainly due to higher marketing expenses (Fig. 3). EBIT margin at 18.3% beat consensus by 30 basis points.

Fig 3

Why are we positive?

As we approach the Q3 earnings season, investor attention has centered on regional dynamics, particularly amid uncertainty around US tariffs. In our assessment, luxury companies noted that demand in the US has remained resilient in recent weeks, suggesting that local momentum may even be strengthening this quarter, with limited evidence of volume pushback to selective price increases. Tariffs remain a key concern for investors given the unknowns around their effect on consumer demand and corporate margins. So, all in all, the sentiment is positive. Moncler will report Q3 sales on 28th October after market close, and we turned positive for the following consideration:

  1. We see support from Q2 results, and more importantly, the company will have a better comp in Q3 than last year. Additionally, the brand is undoubtedly powerful in the upcoming winter season. This provides support to re-rate the company with a buy. Current Q3 trends might be described as weak; however, we do not identify significant negative signs from Google search trends;
  2. Taking advantage of our Italian roots, we gained insight into the new flagship store in Milan. The company is evolving both in retail execution and new products. Moncler's signature puffers remain central to the collection and continue to benefit from strong design innovation. Yet, the brand is increasingly broadening into mid-layer pieces and novel fabric combinations. This strategy helps sustain entry-level price points, while also creating scope for premiumisation with couture-like detailing applied to nylon. Beyond outerwear, women's shirting has emerged as one of the best-selling categories, alongside solid momentum in footwear;
  3. Moncler also worked on a new CRM solution, which helps stores to leverage clients' insights and offer personalised service;
  4. In H1, the company demonstrated solid cost discipline, with operating expenses up 5% year-on-year, and only 1% higher when excluding marketing expenses. In our view, the company retains greater flexibility than peers, supported by its relatively lean structure, with fixed costs accounting for 60% of operating expenses versus a sector average of around 75%;
  5. On the shareholders' basis, LVMH, through a newly formed corporate vehicle, has acquired a 10% stake in Double R (the investment vehicle controlled by Ruffini Partecipazioni Holding), which holds a direct stake in Moncler. In line with the agreements announced a year ago, LVMH has increased its indirect stake in the Milan-listed group to around 4%. This transaction strengthens Moncler's shareholder structure and reinforces alignment between strategic investors.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Although visibility on luxury demand remains limited, we believe Moncler's stepped-up marketing, accelerating organic growth driven by new product launches, and the seasonal tailwind from winter should reaffirm its status as a top pick in the sector. On reverse engineering, we estimate that the Moncler brand increased its pricing by mid-single digits, implying lower volumes in Q2. That said, the company reiterated its sales outlook, and continuing to apply an organic growth of 5.5%, we arrive at a €3.27 billion turnover. A key consideration is how the negative like-for-like could affect the company's fiscal year profitability; nonetheless, we already included FX loss in our sales estimates. The company guided CAPEX at 7% of sales and unchanged marketing expenses, so the core operating margin in H2 should be higher than in H1. This is due to the 150 basis points of higher incidence in marketing expense in H1 due to the timing of events.

At this stage, even applying a lower core operating profit at 28.5%, below Moncler's ambition of a 29-30% level, the company's earnings reached 635 million with an EPS of €2.31. Following a step de-rating, the company trades at 21x P/E on 2025 numbers. We are not accounting for three-year top-line sales and EPS CAGRs of 7% and 6%. At the moment, the luxury P/E projection is set at 24.5x P/E (Fig. 4). Moncler is a high-quality status company with still growth potential, so applying the sector's multiple valuations average, Moncler's shares reached €56.6. We moved our rating to a buy recommendation.

Fig 4

Risks

Moncler's downside risks include: 1) sales normalization in the broader luxury landscape, 2) margin pressure, leaving limited upside to consensus estimates, 3) no valuation multiple re-rating, 4) FX changes, 5) tariff uncertainty in the US and potential decremental sales in China, which is not happening, and 6) lower DTC sales evolution and change in consumer preference. Additionally, the company is subject to higher competition and Stone Island's earnings recovery.

Conclusion

In our last analysis, we highlighted how the company was not immune to the broader sector normalization trends and FX changes. Additionally, Moncler was trading with no more P/E discount. Now, the valuation is not stretched, and we are approaching the winter period. Moncler is again a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Virtus SGA Global Growth Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Returns for Q2 were 12.1% (Gross) and 11.3% (Net) compared to the MSCI ACWI return of 11.5%. Strength in the Mag 7 drove large dispersion between growth and value stocks with the MSCI ACWI Growth outperforming the MSCI ACWI Value by more than 11% in the quarter.
  • The Global Growth portfolio is forecasted to achieve 15% revenue growth and 14% earnings growth annually over the next three years consistent with historical results.
  • Since inception, annualized returns stand at 12.1% (Gross) and 8.8% (Net), compared to 9.3% (MSCI ACWI).
  • Largest Contributors for the quarter were Microsoft (MSFT), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Nvidia (NVDA).
  • Largest Detractors for the quarter were UnitedHealth (UNH), Icon (ICLR), and Apple (AAPL).
  • Initiations – New holdings were introduced in Chipotle (CMG), Waste Management (WM), and Gartner (IT). After quarter end, we initiated a position in Fast Retailing.
  • Liquidations – Apple, Yum! Brands, Icon, and LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) were liquidated in the quarter. Additionally, we sold Novo Nordisk at the beginning of Q3.
  • SGA sees continued opportunity in attractively priced, predictable, cash-generative and growing businesses, which may become more sought after if market volatility increases and growth decreases.

Investment Objective

SGA builds high-conviction portfolios focused on quality growth businesses that are anticipated to achieve consistent mid-teens earnings growth with reduced variability, supported by predictable revenue and cash flow generation. The firm's investment goal is to convert this stable portfolio earnings growth into a return profile designed to protect and reliably compound client wealth over time.

Performance

The Global Growth portfolio (SGAPX) generated 14% revenue growth and 23% earnings growth in 2024 and is forecast for 11% earnings growth in 2025. The portfolio is currently forecast to generate 14% earnings and 15% revenue growth annually over the next three years.

The Global Growth portfolio has generated 12.1% (Gross) and 8.8% (Net) annualized returns since inception, compared to 9.3% and 11.3% for the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI, Net TR) and MSCI ACWI Growth (Net TR), respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, the SGA portfolio returned 11.7% (Gross) and 8.4% (Net) compared to 16.2% and 16.7% for the MSCI ACWI (Net TR) and MSCI ACWI Growth (Net TR), respectively.

Market Context: Continuing Volatility and Policy Shifts

Geopolitically, the quarter began with an uneasy truce in the escalating tariff standoff between the U.S. and its key trading partners. The Trump rhetoric of reshoring incentives and protective tariffs remains the backdrop – particularly targeting goods from China, Mexico, and Canada. The market began to look through the rhetoric as the U.S. started negotiating with trade partners and struck early bilateral trade deals with the UK and China. President Trump announced temporary pauses and rollbacks for some countries and products. Retaliatory measures from trading partners were more measured than anticipated, creating a more stable backdrop.

The economic uncertainty of one quarter ago does not seem to have abated significantly but equity markets have risen near to or exceeded highs seen following the U.S. presidential election. Businesses began adapting supply chains, but fears of a sudden collapse in trade volumes began to subside and investor confidence increased. It is also likely that the adverse bond market reaction undermined Trump’s ability to take a harsher line on trade and hastened a softening of tone. The U.S. dollar has weakened by around 7% against most major global currencies.

In the U.S., inflation moderated after a surprise uptick in Q1, enabling the Federal Reserve to hold rates steady while maintaining a data-dependent stance. Real interest rates remained elevated, but expectations for late-2025 rate cuts began to firm as wage growth decelerated and inventory data softened. In Europe, economic resilience continued, especially in export-heavy markets, supported by stronger-than-expected consumption and easing energy pressures. The European Central Bank signaled caution. Emerging markets posted mixed results. Latin America held up well, with Brazil and Mexico delivering solid industrial output amid moderating inflation, while parts of Asia remained under pressure from capital outflows and weak Chinese consumer confidence.

The S&P 500, which fell 4.3% in Q1, rose by 10.9% in Q2. Non-U.S. equity markets have demonstrated considerable strength, with the Hang Seng Index surging 21.6% year-to-date. Germany’s DAX 40 Index also posted robust returns, rising over 30% year-to-date driven by increased defense spending. The EAFE Index recorded a 19.4% year-to-date gain and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index posted a 15.3% gain. In contrast, U.S. equities, as represented by the S&P 500, showed more moderate returns, gaining only 6.2% year-to-date, weighed down by trade uncertainty and a weaker dollar.

There has been a change in market leadership which reversed many of the moves of the prior quarter. Technology led sectors regained leadership after underperforming in Q1. The Information Technology sector benefited from a more nuanced understanding of AI's commercial trajectory, and the sell-off in high-growth names reversed sharply as earnings and capex data validated long-term theses. The Information Technology sector rose by 23.3% in Q2.

Artificial Intelligence: A Foundational Investment Theme Across the Computing Stack

Our AI investments are concentrated in foundational infrastructure that enables AI advancement rather than pursuing individual algorithmic breakthroughs. This positioning reflects our belief that at this stage, sustainable value creation will accrue to companies providing essential infrastructure, possessing proprietary data assets, or maintaining positions across the computing stack.

Recent developments like DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) optimization techniques underscore that AI computing remains prohibitively expensive for broad adoption. We anticipate dramatic cost reductions over the next several years through hardware advances from companies like Nvidia and software optimization. This trajectory should unlock AI's commercial potential beyond current narrow applications.

Investing Across the Computing Stack; Not in LLMs

Our investment philosophy gravitates toward AI infrastructure and enterprise software beneficiaries rather than Large Language Models themselves, recognizing sustainable value accrues to businesses controlling essential resources and improving productivity. LLMs face rapid obsolescence and unclear monetization paths.

The portfolio's AI exposure spans the computational stack:

  • Design Tools: Synopsys (SNPS) and Arm Holdings provide essential IP and design capabilities
  • Manufacturing: TSMC's (TSM) process leadership remains critical
  • Compute Platforms: Nvidia's data center business offers predictable revenue
  • Cloud Infrastructure: Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) provide computational fabric
  • Enterprise Software: ServiceNow (NOW), Intuit (INTU), and Microsoft translate AI into business outcomes

This diversified approach allows growth projection across layers, building conviction through multiple confirmation points while managing risk through top-to-bottom stack exposure.

Market Dynamics and Competitive Landscape

AI development requires significant capital, data, computing power, and power resources, favoring established businesses such as Alphabet, Amazon, Meta (META), Microsoft, and Nvidia, and creating barriers to early-stage companies which face challenges accessing quality data for model training. These barriers suggest size, scale, deep pockets, and innovation are essential, suiting leading, cash-generative growth companies.

Investment Rationale and Risk Management

Our AI investments are made looking through the same lens of our required investment characteristics: companies demonstrating revenue repeatability, pricing power translating to robust margins funding R&D, and cultures of continuous innovation expanding into growth opportunity. While confident in AI's transformative potential, we maintain disciplined position sizing based on three-to-five-year horizons, avoiding overconcentration while maintaining meaningful exposure.

Looking forward, we believe software will become increasingly sophisticated with intelligent agents performing complex tasks autonomously. The convergence of cheaper compute and improved algorithms should accelerate adoption in robotics and humanoid applications.

The key challenge involves translating AI capabilities into substantial revenue generation beyond cost reduction. Companies showing clear paths to revenue enhancement through AI integration will likely command premium valuations and deliver superior returns.

S&P 500 Sector Relative Performance Percentile

Last 5-Years vs. History, Data Since 1972

Health Care Update

As the chart beside shows, the Health Care sector’s relative performance over the last five-years is near the bottom percentile of data since 1972. In this difficult backdrop, we have also had company specific challenges, which has resulted in Health Care impacting our relative performance significantly.

Specifically, we have seen policies in the U.S. such as the IRA drug price negotiation and the threat of pharmaceutical tariffs or further drug price cuts impact companies that cater to the biopharma industry such as Icon and Danaher. UnitedHealth has also experienced challenges from changes to reimbursement rates from U.S. regulators, and Novo Nordisk has had pipeline disappointments as well as challenges posed by lack of regulatory action against copycatting of its drugs by thousands of smaller direct to consumer outlets.

Recognizing that some of these policies have had a greater impact on near term earnings, we have re-examined our theses and focused client capital where our long-term conviction remains in both the growth potential and growth resiliency.

Largest Contributors

Microsoft

Microsoft has successfully leveraged its portfolio of products into a comprehensive platform centered on an intelligent cloud and intelligent edge, effective in addressing customer needs. The company’s products are sticky with a high cost of switching for customers, providing attractive pricing power. Microsoft’s recurring revenues continue to grow as more products are incorporated into the cloud and edge architecture. Microsoft was a top contributor to performance during the quarter, driven by a strong quarterly report with accelerating growth in its cloud business and continued momentum in AI adoption. Cloud growth accelerated to 35%, almost a 5% beat versus expectations driven by a faster recovery in non-AI workloads and sustained demand for AI infrastructure. Management guided to similar growth for the June quarter, suggesting this momentum may be sustainable over the second half of the year, and in our view, longer. Microsoft’s broad product portfolio, high recurring revenue base, and strong positioning in both productivity software and cloud infrastructure continue to provide resilience and long-term growth potential. We maintained an above-average weight position in the company.

Arm Holdings

Arm Holdings, a leader in the design and licensing of central processing units (CPUs) and related sub systems, was a contributor to performance during the quarter, supported by solid fourth quarter results and a constructive long-term outlook. Revenue grew 34% year-over-year, with licensing revenue up 53% and royalty revenue up 18%. While the company did not issue formal full-year guidance due to macro and tariff-related uncertainty, management indicated that full-year revenue could grow in the high teens, with royalty revenue expected to grow 25-30%. The company emphasized that its growth is increasingly driven by Computer Subsystems (CSS), which enable customers to build custom Arm-based chips more efficiently and with higher royalty rates than traditional CPU cores. Arm’s business model is supported by a two-part revenue stream of recurring licensing and long-tailed royalty revenue, with over 50% of royalties coming from products launched more than a decade ago. The company’s pricing power is reinforced by the difficulty of switching CPU architectures due to software ecosystem dependencies and backward compatibility requirements. Arm is well-positioned to benefit from secular trends in AI, mobile, and edge computing, and its expanding role in enabling custom silicon across industries provides a long runway for growth. We continue to view Arm Holdings as a high-quality compounder with significant long-term potential and built the position to an average weight before trimming on strength.

Nvidia

Nvidia is a leader in design of computer graphics processors (GPUs), system on chips, and related software. GPUs can process many pieces of data simultaneously, making them useful for a myriad of computationally intensive applications. An increasing number of industries are leveraging their technology to automate, enhance, and accelerate their own businesses by incorporating agentic AI, accelerated computing, and recommendation engines. The pace of innovation is extremely rapid in Nvidia’s markets. Nvidia’s shares were strong during the quarter as the company continued to benefit from surging demand for accelerated computing and AI infrastructure. Data center revenue remained the dominant driver of the company’s results, supported by strong growth in inferencing workloads and increasing adoption of generative AI across enterprise and consumer applications. Given concerns around export restrictions to China, CEO Jensen Huang might launch a China-specific product and establish local facilities to offset geopolitical risks. We are less optimistic on sustained momentum there unless there is a long-term geopolitical resolution. The company’s leadership in high-performance GPUs, combined with its full-stack software ecosystem and growing exposure to recurring revenue streams, increase our confidence in its growth trajectory. As a key enabler of end markets like AI and autonomous driving, we believe that the business can continue to grow from here over the next decade and the long-term opportunity remains significant as AI workloads scale and new use cases emerge. We maintained an average weight position.

Largest Detractors

UnitedHealth

UnitedHealth (UNH) is the largest health insurance and health services organization in the U.S. and operates two different businesses, UnitedHealthcare, its medical insurance business, and Optum Health, its service business delivering low-cost, high-quality care. A series of disappointing developments raised questions about the company’s near-term earnings power and execution. The company revised its 2025 guidance downward due to significantly higher-than-expected care activity in its Medicare Advantage (MA) population and a worse-than-anticipated risk profile among newly acquired members. These pressures were most acute in the Optum Health segment, which operates under capitated value-based care arrangements and was disproportionately impacted by the ongoing phase-in of the CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) v28 risk adjustment model. This is a set of rules used to predict healthcare costs for Medicare beneficiaries which will be fully implemented in 2025. The rule change has created near-term pressure on UNH’s margins. While management attributed the earnings shortfall to transitory factors and maintained that the issues are addressable, the magnitude of the miss and the suspension of full-year guidance undermined confidence in the company’s ability to navigate a more challenging regulatory and utilization environment. The abrupt CEO transition added to investor uncertainty, though we view the return of former CEO Stephen Hemsley as a potentially stabilizing force. Hemsley brings deep institutional knowledge and a track record of disciplined execution, and his reappointment may help accelerate operational improvements and restore investor confidence. Separately, reports of a potential DOJ criminal investigation into Medicare Advantage billing practices have surfaced, adding to the headline risk. While the details remain unclear, a recent Special Master recommendation in a related civil case favored UnitedHealth, which may help mitigate some of the near-term overhang. We continue to believe in the long-term opportunity of UnitedHealth’s integrated care model and its leadership position in Medicare Advantage and value-based care.

We have tempered our near-term assumptions to reflect recent execution missteps and external pressures, and under conservative scenarios, we see a compelling investment opportunity. The company’s pricing power is enabled by the scale and data provided through its integrated model, which positions UNH to help its customers lower costs while managing its own profitability. Most of the healthcare insurance and related services are non-discretionary, and a large portion of UNH’s revenues are derived from highly recurring client contracts. As a leader in managed care, we believe the company is exceedingly well-positioned to help address the critical issue of rising healthcare costs in the U.S. and benefit from the continued privatization of government run programs through Medicare Advantage. We lowered the target to a below-average weight position following the stock’s weakness in the quarter, however, we purchased additional shares considering our view of the thesis moving forward.

Icon

Icon was a detractor in the quarter and was liquidated from the portfolio due to a deteriorating near-term outlook and growing uncertainty around the company’s ability to return to its sustainable growth profile. Demand for clinical trials from the company’s pharma and biotech customers has been dampened by a confluence of healthcare policy-related and other factors. First, the Inflation Reduction Act has forced pharma and biotech companies to reprioritize their drug development pipelines to consider the potential for future Medicare price negotiations, including the different timeframes for the start of those negotiations for small molecule drugs versus biologic drugs. Second, uncertainty around the implementation and impact of several potentially new pharma-related government policies, such as Most-Favored-Nation drug pricing, drug industry-specific tariffs, and changes in FDA evidentiary standards/principles for drug approvals, has caused pharma and biotech companies to proceed with their drug development plans in a more slow and cautious manner than usual. Finally, biotech funding, which is relied upon by early-stage drug companies to support their research and development needs, has been weak, which has led small biotech companies to slow their decision-making, delay development projects, and cancel previously planned clinical trials. In addition to some of the near-term pressures, we observed that select large customers were insourcing their clinical trials and using less of Icon’s services, lowering the relevancy and stickiness of the relationship in the long term.

While Icon delivered a modest beat on EBITDA and EPS in the most recent quarter, revenue missed expectations and cancellations remained elevated, and there were no signs of improvement in customer demand. The company lowered full-year EPS guidance, citing the removal of paused vaccine trials, and commentary from management continued to reflect limited visibility into when spending from large pharma and biotech customers might recover. In addition to weak end-market dynamics, Icon has faced scrutiny over the quality of its backlog and a declining backlog burn rate, factors that have raised questions about the reliability of its reported growth metrics. While management maintains that long-term industry fundamentals remain intact, we grew increasingly concerned that because of the impact of government policies and potential regulatory changes, the company may be facing a multi-year period of low or no growth as it works through a backlog that includes delayed projects. With limited near-term catalysts and continued customer caution driven by policy changes, we chose to reallocate capital to higher conviction positions with greater growth visibility.

Apple

We exited the position in early April due to forced attrition as we had better opportunities that were down significantly on tariff concerns. Given the timing of the exit, Apple is one of the largest detractors for the quarter.

That said, one of the key risks to our investment thesis has been the company’s exposure to China and that came into focus this quarter. We have been conservative in our expectations of sales in the country, acknowledging concerns about the impact of slower growth there. Most recently, potential tariffs have created a more challenging environment. With the company’s growth expectations at the lower end of the spectrum in the portfolio, combined with the increased macroeconomic uncertainty, we decided to liquidate the position and reallocate the capital to existing holdings with higher expected growth including AIA Group, Arm Holdings, Alcon, ServiceNow, and Universal Music Group.

Portfolio Activity

We initiated new positions in Waste Management, Gartner and Chipotle. Four positions were liquidated during the quarter – Apple, Yum! Brands, Icon, and LVMH. Additionally, after quarter end we liquidated our position in Novo Nordisk and initiated a new position in Fast Retailing. In Q2, several positions in the portfolio’s Information Technology, Communication Services, and Financials holdings were added to on weakness. In an active quarter there was a lot of trimming and adding activity based on valuation. As technology-related areas performed most strongly, this was the area where we saw most opportunities to trim on strength and conversely the opportunities to add on weakness were mostly outside the technology-related areas.

Purchases

Chipotle

We initiated a new position in Chipotle and built it to an average weight during the quarter. Chipotle operates over 3,700 fast-casual restaurants, primarily in the U.S., offering a focused, customizable menu built around fresh, high-quality ingredients. The company has demonstrated strong pricing power, having successfully passed through multiple rounds of price increases with minimal impact on traffic. Chipotle maintains higher margins despite pricing 10-30% below many fast-casual peers, reinforcing its strong value proposition. Repeatable revenue is supported by its value proposition driving the habitual nature of these purchases and a rapidly growing loyalty program, which now exceeds 40 million members. This platform enables personalized marketing and drives higher frequency and ticket size, contributing to more predictable transaction volumes. Chipotle’s customer base skews toward higher-income consumers, which has helped the brand maintain resilient demand across economic cycles.

Management targets low-teens revenue and mid-teens profit growth, driven by 8-10% annual unit expansion, low to mid-single-digit same-store sales growth, and margin expansion. The company sees a long runway for U.S. growth with a target of 7,000 stores, nearly double its current footprint, and is beginning to scale internationally, with early traction in Canada and Europe. While international margins are currently lower, the domestic playbook is showing signs of success abroad. Chipotle’s unit economics remain compelling, with year-one cash-on-cash returns of ~40% and 60-70% at maturity, supporting continued expansion at attractive returns.

Risks we are monitoring include elevated labor turnover, which could impact store-level productivity and management depth, and the company’s ability to scale its concept internationally, where brand familiarity and unit economics are less proven. Additionally, while food safety practices have improved significantly since prior incidents, maintaining consumer trust remains essential to long-term brand equity. We believe Chipotle’s combination of pricing power, recurring customer engagement, and scalable growth opportunity positions it well to deliver consistent double-digit earnings and revenue growth over the coming years.

Waste Management

A new position was initiated in Waste Management, the leading waste collection and disposal company in North America. Its service is a small but necessary cost for its customers. With its industry having consolidated over time, larger vertically integrated companies like Waste Management have a competitive advantage as landfills have become increasingly scarce. Increasing regulations and capital requirements continue to make it more difficult for sub-scale competitors to operate, making further consolidation in the industry likely. This consolidation and vertical integration lead to strong pricing power for Waste Management. While a long-term beneficiary of these positive trends, we believe Waste Management is at an inflection point in its growth due to new incremental investment opportunities that can accelerate its growth. Most prominently, we see renewable natural gas (RNG) offering high returns as it allows the company to convert the gas generated at landfills into valuable RNG with decarbonization benefits that can be monetized at an attractive premium, primarily through Renewable Identification Number credits. Such investments offer high ROIC’s with strong free cash flow conversion as the bulk of the required investment (the landfill) was already made years ago.

The majority of its revenue is driven by waste collection and disposal with steady volumes growing at a rate 50-66% that of GDP and limited customer churn. Its recycling and RNG business can be more cyclical with revenues partially tied to commodity values, but management has effectively shifted its recycling pricing model to reduce cyclicality and protect margins when commodity prices decline and has taken steps to reduce the volatility of the RNG revenues.

We have strong confidence in the company’s executive management who has done an effective job managing and transforming this business into a more consistent operator. With strong free-cash-flow conversion that is returned to shareholders through a growing dividend and share buybacks, and consistent mid to high-single-digit earnings growth that should accelerate in the years to come, we see Waste Management as an attractive opportunity.

Among the key risks we are monitoring are rising labor costs, compliance with environmental regulations, and RNG demand dynamics.

Gartner

We initiated a new position in Gartner, the leading research and advisory company for IT decision makers globally. The company provides its services to over 15k enterprises today who leverage its 3,000+ research analysts and consultants to determine their strategic priorities and how to best execute them. The business is split into three major categories: Research (84%), Consulting (9%), and Conferences (7%). The Gartner Research platform operates through a subscription model that provides access to its proprietary research and analysts. Executives use Gartner’s research to track emerging trends, understand the strategies and approaches available, and select vendors that are best suited for their needs. The consulting business leverages Gartner’s experienced consultants for those customers seeking more in-depth engagement and project-based advisory services. Gartner’s conferences are considered among the most important events for executive leaders and are generally heavily oversubscribed by enterprises and tech vendors.

Gartner possesses the key business quality characteristics we believe to be important for long-term success. It has strong pricing power as evidenced by its ability to grow pricing 3-4% per year and pass-on larger price increases during inflationary periods as seen in the last few years. About 70% of Gartner’s research subscriptions are multi-year in nature, with total subscriptions representing about 76% of revenues. Client retention for the Research business is around 85%, but net wallet retention is generally above 100% as larger contracts tend to be stickier, and it can upsell higher-fee subscriptions, drive seat growth, and take price over time. Gartner has a long-term growth runway with secular tailwinds due to the growing role of technology in a company’s operations and strategy, and it is uniquely positioned to benefit from those tailwinds due to its strong competitive differentiation.

Among the key risks we are monitoring with Gartner are its performance in budget constrained environments, the DOGE impact on its U.S. Federal Government customers, competition from lower end firms, and any issues which could negatively impact the strength of its brand. Gartner has a demonstrated ability to carefully manage these risks, but they remain areas we will continue to monitor.

Budget constraint concerns provided the recent opportunity to establish a position at an attractive valuation after lower discretionary spend and concerns about federal spending cuts weighed on the stock. We view the discretionary spending weakness as transitory and federal budget cuts may prove less dire than initially feared. Additionally, federal business only represents a small portion of Gartner revenues. We continue to see double-digit earnings growth in the years ahead with strong free cash flow compounding.

Sales

Apple

Apple was sold from the portfolio due to forced attrition to fund higher-conviction opportunities with stronger growth prospects. As noted previously, the company faced increased uncertainty around tariffs hindering an already relatively low growth forecast.

Yum! Brands

We exited our position in Yum! Brands due to forced attrition. The company reported strong quarterly results earlier this year and has managed well against a challenging consumer environment in China and increased price competition in U.S. Quick Service Restaurants. We believe Yum’s value and convenience propositions make the company more resilient to consumer spending pressures and continue to see double-digit earnings growth for Yum over the next three years. However, due to increased uncertainty from macroeconomic and geopolitical issues in China, which represents a mid-teens portion of Yum’s operating profit, and less visibility into potential tariff-related boycotts, we liquidated the position. We used the proceeds to fund a new position in Chipotle which we forecast to have stronger forward growth prospects driven by the company’s U.S. business.

Icon

Icon was sold from the portfolio due to fundamental deterioration to fund higher-conviction opportunities. As described previously, the business continued to face uncertainty around the reliability of backlog conversion, elevated cancellations, and limited visibility into a recovery in customer demand.

LVMH

We exited our position in LVMH during the quarter due to concerns around the company’s reliance on the Chinese consumer, particularly within the soft luxury segment as well as the effects of declining consumer confidence and broader macroeconomic weakness. While we continue to view LVMH as a high-quality business with strong brand equity and scale advantages, recent data suggest a shift in consumer preferences, especially in China, away from soft luxury categories such as leather goods and apparel, and toward hard luxury items like gold jewelry, which are increasingly viewed as stores of value in a more uncertain macro environment. This shift appears to reflect a broader consumer mindset rather than a deterioration in LVMH’s brand equity, with Louis Vuitton maintaining share and other LVMH brands such as Loro Piana and Loewe maintaining solid momentum. However, the company’s exposure to discretionary spending trends in China, combined with macroeconomic headwinds, has created a more challenging setup. With limited near-term catalysts and more compelling opportunities elsewhere in the portfolio, we chose to reallocate capital to higher conviction ideas.

Post-Quarter End Portfolio Activity

Following the end of the quarter, we liquidated our position in Novo Nordisk and initiated a position in Fast Retailing. We sold Novo Nordisk out of the portfolio due to two concerns that made us question the company’s ability to deliver long-term growth.

Firstly, copycatting of Novo’s drugs by direct-to-consumer retail pharmacies is continuing beyond the May 22nd deadline. As background, due to a shortage earlier in 2024 and 2025, the FDA allowed legal copying of Novo’s weight loss drugs (“compounding”) to meet patient demand, with May 22nd marking the end of the authorization as the branded companies have restored sufficient supply. However, the compounders are using a loophole to continue marketing “personalized GLP-1 drugs”, which violates the spirit of the law. One of the retail pharmacies, Empower Pharmacy, is even challenging Novo competitor Eli Lilly’s composition of matter patent on its tirzepatide molecule with the PTAB (Patent Trial and Appeal Board) in an aggressive move. While there are a lot of legal actions to try to rein in the compounding pharmacies by both Novo and Lilly, it appears that this problem of intellectual theft is not likely to go away.

Secondly, at a recent diabetes meeting (American Diabetes Association, June 2025), incremental data regarding Novo’s Phase 3 trials of CagriSema suggested a negative risk/benefit profile. We had previously thought that despite disappointments in the trial data for CagriSema (such as weight loss not meeting the 25% goal), the drug was still safe with a higher efficacy than Novo’s currently marketed drugs. However, new data suggests potential safety concerns. The approval of CagriSema is important to Novo Nordisk, as the company needs to transition its diabetes and obesity franchise away from the semaglutide molecule before patient expiration in major markets in the early 2030s.

Specifically, there were two incidents of suicide out of total 3,008 patients in CagriSema’s Phase 3 trials. Due to the small number of events, it is not clear whether this consists of a true safety signal, and a third-party adjudication committee concluded that the events were not related to the drug. However, the rate of suicide is almost 5x the rate expected from this population in the Phase 3 trials so far. From our experience evaluating relevant precedents, the threshold for a clean approval by the FDA is very high based on the agency’s risk/benefit analysis framework applied to instances where safe, effective alternatives exist. There are many more CagriSema trials that will be reading out before Novo submits CagriSema for approval in early 2026, however we believe there is a new risk that has arisen in the approval pathway for CagriSema.

CagriSema combines cagrilintide (an amylin agonist) with semaglutide (a GLP-1 agonist). As semaglutide is a proven safe molecule (Ozempic and Wegovy), we asked the question whether cagrilintide is potentially causing a safety issue. Review of scientific literature suggests that there is a theoretical basis for the concern. We also noted that there was an increased incidence of depressed mood in a small trial for Lilly’s amylin molecule, eloralintide. Novo Nordisk’s pipeline is broader than CagriSema, however we note that the later stage products, such as amycretin, cagrilintide, and even the earlier stage triple agonist molecule, all share an amylin component, which we are worried could be causing a safety issue. We admit that there is no clear evidence at this point to say that there is a true safety risk. However, putting the puzzle together, we have become aware of a risk - which is broader than one molecule - that could be underappreciated in the pipeline at Novo Nordisk. Together with the continued concerns of compounding, which is a blatant violation of intellectual property and proven to be more difficult to rein in, we have decided to exit the position and reallocate the capital to Fast Retailing, the Japanese retail holding company behind Uniqlo, GU, and other global apparel brands.

Fast Retailing operates as both a manufacturer and retailer, leveraging innovation in product design and marketing to deliver functional, stylish, and affordable clothing. Its vertically integrated supply chain and massive scale allow it to reach customers efficiently and cost-effectively, which supports its ability to maintain attractive price points while preserving margins. Its focus on essential apparel creates a repeatable revenue stream, as customers return regularly to replenish wardrobe staples. The company also has a long runway for growth with proven profitability in Japan, China, and South Korea, traction gaining in Southeast Asia and Europe, and a transformation of its U.S. operations underway. Management has provided long-term guidance for continued margin improvement in North America and Europe, signaling confidence in its international expansion. We saw an attractive opportunity to initiate the position given the stock’s weakness year-to-date.

Outlook

Broader markets experienced volatility once again due to geopolitical and macroeconomic developments. Global markets recovered, led by technology once more, as nerves calmed on President Trump’s tariffs. Returns outside the U.S. were generally stronger than returns for the U.S. market and the weak dollar has clouded investor enthusiasm for the U.S. Despite the uncertainty, quality growth businesses in SGA’s portfolio, like Microsoft, Arm Holdings, and Nvidia performed well. Consistent with our investment philosophy, we took advantage of opportunities generated by market volatility to upgrade the portfolio including initiating new positions in attractively valued companies such as Chipotle, Waste Management, and Gartner and adding to existing positions in AIA Group, Universal Music Group, Alcon, and Aon among others.

We still see great advancements in areas like AI and our portfolio maintains diversified exposure across the entire AI computing ecosystem, reflecting our belief that value creation will occur at multiple layers rather than concentrating in any single segment. This positioning allows us to triangulate growth projections across interconnected components while maintaining conviction in our long-term thesis. Where we encounter areas within the portfolio that are becoming more challenged and companies where we see pricing power or business models under pressure to meet our long-term growth aspirations, we have plenty of ideas to upgrade the portfolio.

Headwinds from stubborn U.S. inflation, deglobalization, high deficits, and high debt levels continue to persist. Ongoing geopolitical tension and conflicts are likely to add to volatility. In a world of broadly slowing growth, we believe our portfolio of quality growth compounders with strong balance sheets, pricing power, and predictability, offers significant value relative to elevated stock market valuations.

Organizational Update

As we communicated to investors in Q3 2024, Alexandra Lee replaced Rob Rohn on SGA’s Executive Committee, which is responsible for running our firm, effective July 1, 2025, joining Kishore Rao and Hrishikesh (HK) Gupta. Rob will remain a member of SGA’s Investment Committee until his retirement on September 30, 2025, at which time he will join SGA’s Advisory Panel which includes external industry veterans as well as retired SGA Founding Principals and serves to preserve institutional knowledge and industry perspective.

data.title: i-80 Gold: Still Cheap Using A Conservative Gold Price
data.description:
Overview

i-80 Gold (NYSE:IAUX)(TSX:IAU:CA) is a Nevada-focused precious metals mining company. The company has a handful of highly prospective assets, all located in Nevada, United States, and a clear development plan that will take i-80 Gold from a developer to a mid-size producer in a few years.

I have covered this company a few times over the last few years, and my prior articles can be found here. My initial position in i-80 Gold was too early, but I have added to that position substantially over the last year due to my conviction in this investment, and this is now one of the larger positions in my portfolio.

i-80 Gold has 5 core mining assets and the Lone Tree autoclave facility, which forms the basis of this growth story. Granite Creek Underground is already in production and is expected to reach commercial production in 2026.

The second underground mine is Archimedes, where the final state permit was received recently for mining activities above the 5100-foot elevation. Permitting the lower elevation is ongoing and expected to be completed by the first half of 2027. Construction has now started, and mining is expected to begin 1-2 years from now.

The ore from both Granite Creek Underground and Archimedes will, in the early years, be processed via a toll milling agreement, until the Lone Tree autoclave refurbishment is completed. A technical study on the autoclave refurbishment is due later this year.

The capital needed for Archimedes and the autoclave refurbishment is roughly estimated to be $400M. Granite Creek Underground is marginally cash flow positive at current prices, even if a large chunk of the margin is eaten up by the toll milling agreement. Some capital is needed to clean up existing liabilities as well, so let's take the total capital figure to ~$500M.

Phase 2 includes the underground mine, Cove, and Granite Creek Open Pit. Phase 3 is the large open pit project Mineral Point. While a lot of the value is in Phase 2 & 3, the main challenge for i-80 Gold is, in my view, completing Phase 1 and reaching 150-200Koz of annual gold production. At that point, funding the remaining projects will be much more manageable in my view.

To get the ~$500M to complete Phase 1 and clean up the balance sheet, the company is looking for a larger debt facility, selling the FAD deposit for a minor amount, a potential royalty on Mineral Point, and possibly also some kind of stream on Lone Tree. To get the larger debt financing in place, feasibility studies are required, which the company is working on. Feasibility studies are due to be released on Granite Creek Underground, Cove, and Granite Creek Open Pit in the first half of 2026. A feasibility study on Archimedes is due first in early 2027.

i-80 Gold did in Q2 of this year announce and close a large bought deal and a smaller private placement for a combined gross value of $184M. The terms of these deals were worse than I would have hoped for, at $0.50, and including a half-warrant at $0.70. This was especially disappointing when management had communicated that the goal was to minimize equity dilution.

My expectation is that the terms would have been far better if the FAD sale and any royalties were announced prior to the equity financings. However, I suspect management overestimated its ability to get those deals through quickly, especially when the balance sheet was more strained.

Due to the multiple assets and $176M in existing debt, i-80 Gold has substantial quarterly expenses of around $25M per quarter, even if that also includes exploration & development expenses.

This leads to a substantial cash burn for a company with only one asset in pre-commercial production. With that said, following the recent financings, the company now has sufficient liquidity until mid-2026. i-80 Gold is before then looking to get the higher-quality technical studies in place, engage with lending providers, and close more structured debt financing.

Valuation

The share price at the time of writing is $0.88, and the fully diluted share count is 1,079 million, as of the 12th of August. That, together with the financials as of Q2 2025, gives us a fully diluted market cap of $949M and an enterprise value of $991M.

The stock price performance of i-80 Gold has been relatively good in 2025, up 89%, which is only slightly below the regular gold mining ETFs that are up just over 100% this year.

However, if we zoom out over the last 3 years, we can see how extreme the underperformance has been for i-80 Gold, which is down 48% while the gold mining ETFs are up around 200% over that period.

Part of the underperformance is justified due to the large equity dilution that occurred recently, but part of it is due to the sentiment in the stock, which explains why the valuation is still so compelling for i-80 Gold.

The combined net present values ("NPVs") for the 5 key projects using the preliminary economic assessments ("PEAs") that were released in Q1 2025 are between $1.6B to $4.5B, depending on what metal prices we use.

The autoclave refurbishment cost is partly deducted in the recent PEAs, but not fully, according to investor relations. So, I have deducted $200M in additional capital cost, to account for the rest of the refurbishment cost. If we compare the current enterprise value with the total estimated net present value, the EV to NPV is 0.37 using a $2,500/oz gold price. The EV to NPV is as low as 0.23, using a gold price of $2,900/oz, which is 20% below the latest spot price, I might add.

Risks

There is little doubt there is value in i-80 Gold's assets, but there are risks as well. The very near-term liquidity concerns have decreased substantially, but the company still needs somewhere around $500M over the coming year to clean up existing liabilities on the balance sheet and get the Lone Tree autoclave facility up and running.

This is the main risk in my view, where I think raising ~$500M is achievable with feasibility studies in place, with minimal equity dilution. It is worth remembering that all assets are located in Nevada, United States, which is one of the best mining jurisdictions in the world.

There are permitting risks with some of the assets as well, even if these are all past producing assets, which mitigates that risk some. So, I am not overly concerned that the assets will not get permitted, even if potential delays might cause issues along with an extended balance sheet.

Even if the assets are financed and permitted, you still have execution risk as well. The staged development approach is mitigating that risk some. The larger operation, Mineral Point, will be developed later on, when the company is expected to be generating substantial cash flow from the Phase 1 & 2 operations. So, the balance sheet & liquidity risks will likely be substantially lower at that point.

As with all mining companies, there is also commodity price risk. However, as illustrated in the valuation section, i-80 Gold has an extremely compelling valuation with a gold price 20% below the latest spot price. So, provided the gold price can remain relatively constructive, there is less commodity price risk than for other gold miners that might be more fully valued using the latest spot price.

Conclusion

Overall, there is a lot to like with i-80 Gold. The stock price has started to re-rate over the last month, which I think will continue, even if it is unlikely to go in a straight line. Looking at a 2–3-year time frame, I think few gold mining companies offer a better risk reward using a $3,000/oz gold price.

An investment in i-80 Gold is not without risk, though, but I think the upside potential clearly outweighs the downside risk. The fact that i-80 Gold has all its assets in Nevada and has an autoclave facility should, in my view, warrant a premium valuation, which is far from reality today. I rate i-80 Gold a strong buy.

The company has delivered very strong drill results over the last few years, from Granite Creek Underground and Cove in particular, which likely offer substantial growth potential over time. This is not reflected in the current valuation either.
