data.title: Cenovus Energy: A Great Canadian O&G Company To Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Cenovus Energy (NYSE:CVE) (TSX:CVE:CA) is currently one of the biggest Canadian oil and gas companies with a diverse portfolio of assets, starting with oil sands all the way to refining and marketing. With a previous analysis covering Canadian Natural Resources, I want to see how Cenovus differs and if it can be a better investment going forward.

CVE also announced that it was planning to take over MEG Energy, and with its excellent M&A track record, this could prove to be yet another transformative deal for the energy company.

Operation Overview

CVE differentiates its operation between Upstream and Downstream with a near 50/50 split in terms of segment-specific revenues.

Regarding the upstream segment, it is the current backbone of CVE with a nice mix of oil sands, offshore production and conventional resources, providing a strong, diverse production stream. In the latest quarter, the company produced 766MBOE/d and the segment generated C$29.775B over the past twelve months.

  • For Oil Sands, CVE has great assets in Alberta, mainly consisting of the Foster Creek and Christina Lake, which are cornerstone assets and among the largest and most efficient thermal projects in the industry. These rely on the SAGD (steam-assisted gravity drainage) technology that CVE pioneered, which helps to extract bitumen from reservoirs that are too deep for mining. Currently, CVE still has 35 years' worth of reserves with required sustaining capital standing between $7-$9 per barrel.
  • CVE also has a significant presence in conventional sources of O&G with a set of production basins in Alberta and British Columbia that provide a mix of crude, NGLs, and nat gas.
  • Regarding offshore production, CVE operates in two key regions that allow a relative geographic diversification. First, Asia Pacific with the Liwan Gas Project, and second, in the Atlantic with the White Rose field.

For the downstream operations, it focuses on processing the company’s heavy crude into higher-value refined products as well as third-party crude. CVE posted a downstream throughput of 666 Mbbls/d during Q2. Some key refineries include the Lloyminster facility as well as the Lima Refinery in the US that provides flexibility to process various types of crude. The company has been focusing on improving its crude utilization with a target improvement of 13% since 2023, with measures such as enhancing preventative maintenance programs as well as reliability improvements in key refineries. The company has seen record Canadian refining rates through the past 6 months. The segment was responsible for C$32B in the LTM.

Current Dynamics

A big surprise came in late August when Cenovus announced that it is pursuing the acquisition of oil sands producer MEG Energy, which has long-life assets adjacent to the Christina Lake operations. Cenovus made a cash and stock bid of C$7.9B on the back of Strathcona launching a hostile bid of C$5.93B which started a bidding war for MEG. The board of MEG has concluded that the best option would be to be acquired by Cenovus at a 27.9% premium to MEG’s last close before Strathcona launched its initial bid. Strathcona has since revised its offer to a value of around C$30.86, though the board continues to prefer CVE’s offer as it believes that it fails to address or adequately compensate for the risks embedded in Strathcona shares.

I believe that the deal could be a good strategic move for Cenovus as it will drastically increase the integrated company’s reserves and production of oil sands, creating a consolidated position in the region and producing over 720,000 Bpd. CVE also expects over $400MM of annual synergies per year starting in 2028. Though it will tilt CVE’s energy mix more towards Alberta’s Oil Sands, which are higher-cost and more carbon-intensive. Furthermore, I wouldn’t bet against the company that snatched Husky Energy for C$3.9B.

CVE also announced the sale of its 50% interest in the WRB refining JV to its partner Phillips 66 for around C$1.9B which is another step towards the company’s strategy of focusing and controlling the core of its assets. The proceeds will mainly go towards debt reduction as well as improving shareholder returns. With the current refining environment, I see the move as a positive as well as it will allow CVE to have more direct control over its performance and will allow it to better integrate its refining network with its heavy oil production.

I also want to highlight the impact of the Trans Mountain Pipeline, which has become operational in mid-2024 and became crucial for CVE and other Canadian producers that have now gained better access to international markets, outside of the reliance on the US market. This should improve the competitiveness of Western Canadian Select with better pricing for producers compared to WTI as it reaches higher demand markets, strongly benefiting producers such as Cenovus.

Valuation

Cenovus has an in-line valuation with its peers, as I see nothing extraordinary in terms of undervaluation potential compared to the industry. CVE has a P/E Non-GAAP of 15.72, higher than peers such as Imperial Oil or Suncor, while its EV/EBITDA ranks towards the lower end. Furthermore, CVE currently has a healthy FCF yield of 7% though slightly down from the past years, as crude prices dipped from their 2022 highs. With the potential of enhanced FCF with the MEG deal and the improved market access, I could see the company's valuation multiples going higher as the outlook for Canadian O&G companies is certainly brighter than a couple of years back.

Growth metrics presented in the following graph can be biased as it compares to the peak crude prices in 2022. Otherwise, CVE has grown tremendously since its upstream-only days, especially with the merger of Husky which added downstream capabilities to the overall company. With the potential MEG Energy deal, I believe that CVE is poised to grow even further with a bet on concentration and optimization. This will create a more powerful and efficient oil sands integrated giant, which will double down on its expertise other than trying to cover all grounds of the energy supply chain. The landscape for Canadian energy has been heightened by several factors, such as the TMX pipeline and oil sands offer upsides to an integrated O&G company.

Margins have also been heightened since the Husky deal, as the company has been able to capitalize on higher-value transformation. This has translated to a gross margin nearly doubling from 9.2% in 2015 to now 18.3%. Though CVE’s margins are generally lower than its peers, as it uses the SAGD method that is highly effective but also highly energy-intensive and costly on a per-barrel basis. Due to this, CVE is also dependent on natural gas prices as it acts as a feedstock, resulting in higher costs of goods sold. If natural gas prices increase drastically compared to crude, it will see its margins squeezed. Analysts certainly think that the Canadian natural gas market could strengthen, resulting in lower gross margins for CVE. Furthermore, the company’s refinery business has been operating in tough market conditions as overcapacity and reduced demand grip the industry. Though, since the 2021 Husky deal, CVE has been able to post strong ROIC and ROE, and I’m sure that this will further increase if the company can close the MEG deal.

Cenovus also pays a relatively healthy dividend at a yield of 3.15%, which has been growing quite incredibly over the past 5 years at a rate of 41.51% CAGR. CVE also mentioned that it can sustain its base dividend at prices of $45 WTI, which leaves a significant safety net. CVE is planning to grow its base dividend capacity to C$2B. This is part of the strategy of returning 100% of its excess funds flow to shareholders when net debt is below its target of C$9B. Adding to that, the company does accretive share buyback, under the umbrella of this strategy, with around C$819MM in total returned to shareholders during Q2.

The O&G company has done a fantastic job at reducing its leverage over the years with many debt-related metrics improving over the years such as its Net Debt/EBITDA falling to 0.9 and its EBITDA/Interest Expense increasing to one of the healthiest levels in the industry at 20.5. As CVE completes the deal for MEG, the company will certainly increase its debt level, potentially reducing its shareholder return in the short term, though I expect it to be only transitory.

Risks

CVE is, at the end of the day, highly dependent on commodity prices that are inherently volatile. The company has a robust balance sheet that can withstand prices of $45 WTI, but a sharp downturn could severely hamper the company’s financial performance. There is also the WCS differential that trades at a discount to the WTI benchmark, with a potential widening of this causing harm to CVE’s operation, though I do not see it as likely.

CVE also mainly operates in Canada which tends to be less friendly towards carbon-intensive enterprises compared to the US. Canada imposes policies such as the Carbon Tax that have reduced overall profitability for the company, and if they were to increase, this could constrain the company’s future production.

Conclusion

CVE has a great track record in terms of accretive deals at opportune times, and I believe that this will be the same with the MEG deal. With the company currently weathering headwinds relatively well with still strong FCF generation, I don’t see how it will be able to capitalize as refinery dynamics normalize and it optimizes its portfolio. I rate Cenovus Energy as a buy

data.title: Monster Beverage: Momentum Brewing, But Shares Are Fairly Priced
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I give Monster Beverage Corporation (NASDAQ:MNST) a hold because, even though the company has a strong balance sheet and sales momentum going into Q3, along with high analyst expectations for the long-term future, management noted in their last conference call that they are preserving cash for their share buyback program, possibly because Q3 could be slower due to tariffs having a modest impact on earnings or because the company could be waiting for better entry points for the purchase of shares and, therefore, expect a dip in price in the near-term future. Both the intrinsic and relative valuations I give below also have the company fairly priced or slightly overvalued from their current levels, solidifying my belief that the stock is a hold.

A Personal Anecdote

I've been wanting to do analysis on Monster for quite some time, mostly because I drink a lot of Monster Energy, specifically the original green version. I am quite the caffeine fiend, and the kick that I get after drinking 160mg worth of caffeine always hits the spot if I'm not in the mood for coffee. And I'll be honest: Until I opened up the company's latest 10-K, I had no idea that Monster had such a deep lineup of products. So, let's break down how the company makes their money.

Revenue Segments

The vast majority of the company's sales come from their primary segment of revenue: the Monster Energy Drinks segment, representing 91.6% of net sales for the fiscal year ended December 31st, 2024. This segment of revenue is made from selling the company's flagship Monster Energy drink product, as well as the Java Monster Coffee + Energy drinks, the Nitro, Rehab, Reign, and Bang line of energy drinks, as well as their Monster Tour line of water.

The company's Strategic Brands segment, made up of the energy drink brands acquired from The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE:KO), as well as other energy drink brands, totaled 5.8% of net sales, while the Alcohol Brands segment represented 2.3% of all net sales and the Other segment represented 0.3% of all net sales.

Q2 Balance Sheet Snapshot

Let's dive into the company's balance sheet found in their latest 10-Q:

The company has $8.73 billion in assets, of which $1.93 billion is in cash and cash equivalents, and $1.54 billion is in liabilities—none of which is long-term debt. As a matter of fact, the company's long-term debt, noted in their 10-K, was paid off as of April 2025.

In other words, 85.02% of the company's balance sheet consists of assets, while 14.98% consists of liabilities, representing an excellent assets-to-debt ratio. The company also has a strong equity-to-debt ratio of 82-18 and has enough cash to pay off all liabilities.

And dividing the company's current cash and cash equivalents by their outstanding shares of common stock gives us a cash per share of $1.97; this is not very impressive given that the company currently trades at $66.16 (usually I look for a cash per share totaling at least 10% of the company's share price), but it's not a surprise either, given that cash only represents 22.07% of total assets. Nevertheless, this is still a very impressive balance sheet.

Q2 Earnings

Looking at my notes from the company's Q2 earnings call transcript, Monster's latest earnings were undoubtably bullish. Hilton H. Schlosberg, Vice Chairman and Chief Executive Officer for Monster, summarized the major highlights succinctly in the following statement:

Our record quarterly net sales crossed the $2 billion threshold for the first time in the company's history. In addition, the percentage growth rates in reported gross profit, operating income, net income and earnings per share all outpaced our growth rate in net sales.

Since the company had record net sales, this meant an 11.1% increase YoY with net income (earnings) per diluted share up 21.1% YoY to $0.50 from last year's $0.41.

However, more than anything, what I would like to do now is to give the company a valuation through a Peter Lynch earnings line to measure whether the company is overvalued or undervalued:

Valuation 1: The Peter Lynch Earnings Line

Below is a YChart with four inputs: The first is the company's stock price, the second is the company's diluted EPS for the trailing twelve months [TTM], the third is the company's current P/E ratio, and the fourth is the company's 10-year median P/E ratio.

As we see, Monster is currently trading at 41.11 times earnings, and over the last ten years, Monster's median (or normal) market valuation has been 36.91 times earnings. If we multiply the company's diluted TTM EPS by this normal market valuation, the company's Peter Lynch earnings line sits at $59.43, meaning the stock is currently trading at approximately 11.36% above its earnings line. The company is also growing at a 15% growth rate, and, to me, a 15% grower with a P/E of 41 hardly screams a buy; if anything, this indicates a clear hold.

Nevertheless, analyst estimates paint a more overall bullish picture for the company going forward. Below is a snapshot of consensus EPS estimates from Seeking Alpha going as far as December 2027:

These EPS consensus estimates have the company's forward P/E falling well below the market median valuation by 2026. If these estimates hold, the stock would go from its current overvaluation to undervaluation relative to its 10-year median. All of this, of course, depends on whether the company's earnings continue to meet or exceed analyst expectations, which could happen given all of the company's headwinds noted in their latest earnings report. The best way to know for sure is to try to gauge what Q3 earnings will look like.

Looking Ahead to Q3

Just being honest, it's hard to know what Q3 earnings will look like. For starters, the latest Q2 earnings report noted excellent July sales, meaning plenty of momentum going into Q3. As noted in the conference call by Schlosberg:

We estimate that July 2025 sales on a non-foreign currency adjusted basis, were approximately 24.3% higher than the comparable July 2024 sales and 24.9% higher on a non-foreign currency adjusted basis, excluding the Alcohol Brands segment.

This is nothing short of good news since the company is still carrying massive momentum from their record Q2. However, there were some bearish signals to make note of.

First, Schlosberg did warn of headwinds going into Q3, specifically referring to tariffs having a "modest impact." When he was questioned about the modest tariff pressure, Schlosberg responded with the following.

We don't give guidance. So I've got to be careful what I say otherwise. I might get into trouble here with the lawyers. But we do see some modest pressures coming from tariffs in Q3. And in Q4, if the price increase has not materialized, but we think it will. We will see some reduction in -- through tariffs. But we do believe that the price increase will go some way towards overcoming that.

The tariffs referred to by Schlosberg impact the company's aluminum, though he noted that Monster Beverage has a hedging strategy in place, meaning the company is taking steps to reduce risks from price swings.

But what was, to me, another bearish signal for the immediate near-term future, was that the company did not purchase any of their outstanding shares. As seen in the YCharts below, as of the last ten years, the company has been on a mission to buy back shares, which is one of the things I most appreciate as an investor. The company has $500 million remaining to repurchase shares under their previously authorized repurchase program; yet, during Q2, no shares were purchased.

There are two ways to interpret this: Either management may be preserving their cash in preparation for a slower Q3, or the company could be waiting for better entry points and, therefore, expect a dip in price in the near-term future. I'm assuming the latter is the reason, but I can't be too certain; hence my uncertainty in trying to predict what Q3 earnings will look like.

Thesis Risks

Since my investment thesis is that the company is a hold at its current levels, I will give two thesis risks: one being that I am wrong and the company's stock is actually a sell and the other that the company's stock is a buy.

Let's start with the bearish case and say that the company is a sell. Probably the most bearish take I could gather from their latest earnings call is the fact that Monster's growth may not equal maximum corporate profits (i.e. even though sales are on the rise, this is coming from lower-cost regions and brands). For example, the company's best region in Q2 was APAC (i.e. Asia-Pacific), followed by EMEA (i.e. Europe, the Middle East, and Africa), with LATAM (i.e. Latin America) and the U.S. placing last. Combine this with the modest tariff impact for aluminum potentially slowing down Q3 and possibly Q4 earnings, and I could see earnings steadily decreasing and lagging behind other beverage companies for Q3 and Q4, thereby lowering the stock price in the short-term.

The bull case, which I believe is likelier than the bear case above and, again, going off of the latest earnings call, depends on whether the company is able to sidestep the modest tariff impact through their hedging strategy and successfully increase their prices. As Schlosberg stated in the latest earnings call:

So turning to gross margins. I'm always been very passionate about gross margins and where the gross margins can end up in the company. ... And in Q4, if the price increase has not materialized, but we think it will.

In other words, the company is expecting price increases by Q4, the logic behind their price increases being the company's belief that they "regard energy drinks as an affordable luxury," hence why the company continues "to review opportunities for price increases domestically and internationally." Successful price increases could override the bearish worry noted above that Monster's growth may not equal maximum corporate profits, since international locations would also be affected by price increases, and could definitely lead to another quarterly record earnings in the short-term.

What I would like to do now is to close the article by focusing on the company's free cash flow [FCF] and earnings quality and perform a discounted cash flow [DCF] to put an exact price tag on the company.

The Annual Free Cash Flow and Earnings Quality

Let's start with the company's annual free cash flow, taking the numbers from Monster's latest 10-K:

  • Cash provided by operating activities was $1.93 billion, an increase of roughly 12.3% from last year's $1.72 billion.
  • Capital expenditures, calculated from adding the company's purchases of property and equipment to the company's additions to intangibles, remained very low at only $306.43 million, an increase of roughly 30.5% from last year's $234.72 million.

The difference between these two numbers above results in an annual free cash flow of $1.62 billion, an increase of roughly 9.4% from last year's $1.48 billion.

And with $420 million more coming in from cash from operations than in reported net income, the company has, undoubtably, high quality earnings.

Valuation 2: An Equity-Focused DCF

Since Monster Beverage is a company with stable cash flows that has had consistent revenue and cash flow growth for over a decade now, my second valuation metric will be an equity-focused DCF, and I will use the annual free cash flow above of $1.62 billion as my base-year FCF.

I will use a long-term growth rate for five years of 15.1% (This number comes from LSEG Data & Analytics Stocks Plus Report, expecting a five-year growth rate of 15.1%, which is also in line with Seeking Alpha's three-to-five year long-term growth rate of 15.08%).

Applying the Growth Rate to Monster's FCF

Taking the company's base-year annual free cash flow and multiplying it with its growth rate over the next five years gives us the following numbers below:

  • Year 1 (2025): $1.86 billion
  • Year 2 (2026): $2.15 billion
  • Year 3 (2027): $2.47 billion
  • Year 4 (2028): $2.84 billion
  • Year 5 (2029): $3.27 billion

Acquiring the Discount Rate

The next step is to get the discount rate. I will do so using the capital asset pricing model [CAPM] with the following numbers:

  • A risk-free rate of 4.06% is taken from the yield on U.S. securities maturing in ten years.
  • We'll use an expected market return of 10% taken from the stock market's average annual return.
  • A beta of 0.57 taken from the sector beta for the beverage industry.

Using these three numbers, we can calculate the company's discount rate by plugging them into the CAPM formula below:

  • 0.0406 + (0.1 - 0.0406) x 0.57 = 0.074458 x 100 = 7.45% Discount Rate

Discounting Future Cash Flows to Their Present Value

Now we'll calculate the present value of each and every future cash flow using the present value formula: future value ÷ (1 + discount rate) ^ year, with the results shown below:

  • Year 1 (2025): $1.73 billion
  • Year 2 (2026): $1.86 billion
  • Year 3 (2027): $1.99 billion
  • Year 4 (2028): $2.13 billion
  • Year 5 (2029): $2.28 billion
  • Years 1 to 5 (Total): $9.99 billion

To get the terminal value of the company, we'll take the company's FCF in Year 5 of $3.27 billion (before calculating its present value) and apply a conservative terminal growth rate of 3% into our formula below:

  • FCF in 2029 × (1 + terminal growth rate) = Year 6 FCF (2030)
  • 3.27 × (1 + 0.03) = $3.37 billion

We'll now divide the company's FCF in Year 6 (2030) of $3.37 billion by the discount rate of 7.45% subtracted by the terminal growth rate of 3%:

  • $3.37 billion ÷ (0.0745 − 0.03) = $75.73 billion
  • Present Value of Terminal Value: $52.87 billion

Adding up the total present value of all future cash flows years 2025 to 2029 to the present value of the firm's terminal value yields an equity value of $62.86 billion.

Getting the Intrinsic Value Per Share

Finally, to get the intrinsic value per share, we'll divide the company's equity value by the company's current number of common stock, per the company's latest 10-Q, of 976,428,189 to get an intrinsic value of a rounded $64.38, which means that the company is currently trading 2.76% above its intrinsic value.

Conclusion

Overall, Monster has solid fundamentals. The only thing stopping me from giving the company a buy rating is its current valuation. If I really wanted to purchase shares, my target entry would be $59. Anything less, and I think you're getting in at a good price for a company with a bright future.

data.description:
EQL fast facts

ALPS Equal Sector Weight ETF (NYSEARCA:EQL) was launched on 07/06/2009 and tracks the NYSE® Equal Sector Weight Index. EQL has 11 ETF holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.65%, and a net expense ratio of 0.27%. It is a mid-size ETF, with $558 million in assets under management (“AUM”) and a 3-month average daily dollar trading volume of $1.1 million. The sponsor, ALPS Advisors, is an asset management boutique offering ETFs, mutual funds, closed-end funds, and variable insurance trusts.

EQL strategy

As described by ALPS Advisors,

The Underlying Index is an index of ETFs comprised of all active Select Sector SPDR® ETFs in an equal weighted portfolio.

The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis in March, June, September, and December. In summary, the fund invests in 11 ETFs representing all the GICS sectors. Its turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 7%. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

data.title: France's Business Climate Holds Steady Despite Political Uncertainty
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

Stability masks underlying fragilities

Despite ongoing political instability, France’s business climate indicator held at 96 in September for the fourth consecutive month, below its long-term average. The survey was conducted after the confidence vote announcement, meaning business leaders' responses already reflect the political turbulence. In this context, the indicator's stability is somewhat reassuring.

The services sector saw a two-point rebound, driven mainly by improved assessments of past activity. However, general expectations fell sharply. The improvement was entirely due to the "information communication" sub-sector, which accounts for 29% of services. All other sub-sectors reported declines. In manufacturing, sentiment deteriorated due to weaker production and declining order books. Retail sentiment hit its lowest level since April 2022 amid worsening outlooks and rising uncertainty. The construction sector remained stable, while wholesale trade saw a modest improvement.

The most concerning development is the employment climate, which fell by two points due to reduced hiring plans in services. At 93, it now stands at its lowest level since January 2015, excluding the pandemic period. This sharp decline signals a likely slowdown in economic activity in the coming months.

Growth outlook dims as uncertainty rises

After a solid start to the third quarter, the risk of a marked slowdown in late Q3 and Q4 is high. Household consumption is expected to remain subdued, constrained by a weaker labour market and uncertainty surrounding the 2026 budget. With the savings rate at its highest since the late 1970s, a rebound in spending after a weak first half seems unlikely. Budget uncertainty may also prompt businesses to delay hiring and investment decisions.

All in all, following a moderately positive third quarter, stagnation is likely in the fourth quarter. We forecast GDP growth of 0.6% in 2025 and 0.8% in 2026, down from 1.1% in 2024.

Budget challenges intensify

This weak economic momentum will worsen France’s fiscal outlook. The budget proposal by François Bayrou was based on a 1.2% growth assumption for 2026. In this context, the task facing the new Prime Minister, Sébastien Lecornu, of forming a government and passing a budget is even more complex.

The political discussions of the last few days clearly show that the negotiations are likely to continue for a long time. There has been no concrete progress, no sign of an imminent agreement. In fact, quite the opposite. The likelihood of reaching an agreement on the government and the budget that would secure the support of the entire centrist bloc, the Socialist Party on the left and Les Républicains on the right, appears very low, in a very tense social climate. And, even if the new prime minister manages to form a government and pass a budget, the deficit for 2026 is likely to remain well above 5% next year.

The €44bn savings target proposed by Bayrou appears to have been abandoned; the idea of ​​aiming for €35bn is circulating, but without any concrete steps. France is on track to remain the eurozone’s worst fiscal performer in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

The photo below is from BRBI BR Partners' (NASDAQ:BRBI) 2021 earnings presentation. In addition to being a little nostalgic for me, it proves that I have known the company for some time, since around its IPO in Brazil in 2021. And now, it is time for the IPO on Nasdaq, with the aim of attracting foreign investors who recognize the bank's potential.

Although there are uncertainties surrounding the thesis, it combines the two main attractive factors in equity investment, growth and income, and here I am not talking about dividend growth, but rather a company that, in addition to having a double-digit dividend yield, continues to have very high potential.

My take is that, in addition to this long-term potential, the BR Partners thesis is also an “undiscovered play,” and still has a lot to capture in terms of coverage in the US and Brazil, and consequently, improve liquidity and re-rating of the valuation.

That said, I assign a buy rating to BRBI stock and consider it to be among my best GARP ideas.

A Little About BR Partners

I believe that many are unfamiliar with BR Partners. It is an investment bank from Brazil, and despite being both a “recent” IPO (in 2021) and a small bank ($350 million market cap), it is already operationally robust and has a complete advisory portfolio, as the table below shows.

In the image below, you can see the company's history, from its founding by Ricardo Lacerda to its IPO, which is where the company gained more firepower to expand its products more rapidly. It is interesting to note that not only has the portfolio (image above) evolved, but many efforts and advances have been made in areas such as liquidity/governance, as illustrated by the follow-on and Nasdaq listing.

Discussing wide moats for this type of niche is a little more difficult and debatable, but I believe that BR Partners has built some relevant ones. The first is its ownership culture, and the acquisition and, above all, retention of talent in this industry is essential. The company already has elaborate models for compensation, partnership, and alignment of long-term interests.

Linked to this is the bank's reputation, which has already proven itself as an independent player, having advised on several important deals in Brazilian industry and now building an important network of contacts, often creating something analogous to a switching cost, due to the length of its relationship with some customers.

In turn, there is synergistic potential between the areas. The ecosystem is already complete, and therefore, the company can benefit in various scenarios and act in a complementary manner. For example, after selling a company, the customer may be interested in wealth management, just as a wealth management customer may be interested in IPOs, PE, and other types of operations.

This complete portfolio also mitigates one of the main risks of the thesis, which is the complexity of the Brazilian macroeconomy. Although we are not seeing IPOs in the market, and high uncertainty and high interest rates are restricting M&A, the company is still able to sustain its revenue through debt issuance (Capital Market).

Financials and Key Info About BR Partners

In the last earnings call, the bank made it clear that this is one of the worst moments for investment banking, especially for M&A. Still, the financials are far from bad. Between 2020 and 2024, revenue showed a CAGR of 24%, and for 2025 it will likely be closer to flat. Revenue per managing director has recently remained at R$45 million, even in a more challenging period.

It is worth mentioning that the compensation ratio in 2024 was just under 25%, and the company's limit is 30%. Meanwhile, all of its peers had an indicator above 50%. This is very important, since revenue per MD is not that far off, surpassing companies such as Moelis & Co. (MC) and Houlihan Lokey (HLI).

Below, you can see how other key indicators have been performing recently. The YoY decline in revenue can be largely attributed to lower IB and CM revenue, as well as lower capital revenue in the period, while Treasury Sales & Structuring and Wealth Management, which are less representative, continue to grow at double digits (with WM standing out with 32% YoY growth).

Note that although it is a small company, ROAE is exceptional, remaining above 20% and at times approaching the ROAE of BTG Pactual, which is infinitely larger, with revenue more than 50 times greater. Another point to highlight is the Basel ratio. The indicator is above 20%, well above the Brazilian Central Bank's minimum requirement of 11%. This shows both conservatism in the balance sheet and room for growth.

This is especially important because part of BRBI' business is “balance sheet as a service.” BR Partners can place part of the debt it issues in its portfolio, for example, to assist with liquidity. In other words, sometimes, in addition to earning fees from the CM process, the company can also earn by retaining part of the security.

This means that BRBI shareholders are also indirectly exposed to Brazil's fixed income market. For example, BR Partners recently coordinated the issuance of a bond by Dotz S.A., which pays DI + 8% per annum and matures in 2029. “DI” is only a few bps lower than the Selic Rate, which is 15% per year, i.e., it is an extremely high-yield security. I will not go into whether or not it is a good security, since much of Dotz's history is a net loss, and only recently in Q2 did the company manage to present a more significant net income.

Triggers & Growth Opportunities for BR Partners

To understand the bank's main growth opportunities, it is necessary to understand the context of the Brazilian market. Its main activities, such as M&A, are directly affected by Brazil's high interest rates, as well as by the landscape of uncertainty.

The Selic Rate (Brazil's FFR) is at 15% per year, an extremely restrictive level and one of the highest in recent history, as you can see in the chart below.

One might think that this high risk-free rate comes with a high inflation outlook, but that is not quite the case. Despite the uncertainties and debates, the outlook is for inflation (IPCA) to end 2025 at 4.8%. This creates one of the highest real interest rates in the world, which consequently slows down the economy and completely changes risk appetite.

The consequence for the BR Partners market is obvious. Even with so many companies issuing debt at such a high cost of debt, and some M&As being done, this macroeconomic scenario is under pressure and far from its full potential. There have been no IPOs on the Brazilian stock exchange for years.

This creates pent-up demand for BR Partners, which is one of the well-positioned banks in this market, to capture when the cycle improves. The Selic rate is expected to continue falling in the coming years, although still at high levels, but it is well known that Brazil is a cyclical country. When the perceived fiscal risk decreases and interest rates fall to a more acceptable level, companies that have postponed their IPOs should “rush” to complete them within an acceptable window, and the same is true for debt issuance, M&As, and even risk appetite and mark-to-market gains for other revenues.

In addition to the turnaround in the macroeconomic scenario, there are several opportunities for growth in the Brazilian market and BR Partners' market share. The company already has a significant market share in some markets, such as 27% of the independent advisors market and M&A, but in my view, there is still plenty of room for players like BR Partners to gain ground as the market becomes less fragmented.

The most emblematic is Wealth Management, where the company has only 2.4%, so it has the potential to boost this market share in the long run. The market is no longer small, as BTG Pactual's revenue of more than R$4 billion from wealth management and personal banking alone shows, but it still has a lot of room to mature as factors such as economic cycles, financial education, and the like play out in the Brazilian economy.

In other words, I believe that the prospects for top-line growth and, consequently, for value creation for the bank are positive for the next years, not to mention optionalities such as specific niches that the bank may begin to explore, internationalization, and other trends such as possible waves of privatizations and other state assets, depending on the governments in the coming years.

The Main Risks for BR Partners

In my view, there are three main risks to BR Partners' thesis. The first is what I mentioned above as the potential held back by the Brazilian market. Although there are signs that interest rates will fall over time, there is no guarantee that the fiscal scenario will improve. It is not impossible that worse cycles, such as the one currently affecting the country, will continue for some time or even slow down a little more. In this scenario, the company may lose revenue and margin and even see its multiples compressed given the expectations and uncertainty surrounding its growth.

The second risk, which I believe is more important, is competition and concentration. Although Brazil is large and has several companies and sectors that make the IB and CM markets more heated, the big players matter a lot. In other words, if relationships with strategic clients are lost, this could substantially hurt revenue, and this is closely linked to intense competition in this sector, ranging from big players such as BTG Pactual to smaller boutiques such as BRBI.

The third latent risk to the business is its dependence on talent. The bank has grown well in recent years, but the number of MDs and partners is still small. Therefore, if any key senior staff leave, retire, or similar, this could have some level of impact on the relationship network.

Finally, it is worth mentioning some more generic risks, such as the exchange rate risk in Brazil (and you will see that in the section below, I will do the valuation in BRL), and the greater volatility due to it being not only a Brazilian stock, but a small cap with more limited liquidity.

The good news is that the company is actively working to resolve this second point, as illustrated by ADR's Nasdaq listing. The company aims to expand its shareholder base and improve liquidity and has spoken with a number of funds focused on small caps and emerging markets. It is worth mentioning that two American small-cap funds have begun to build positions. With this greater institutional and American visibility, perhaps the valuation gap compared to peers may narrow.

BRBI’s Valuation: GARP or Deep Value?

Based on revenue and margin trends, I believe that BR Partners’ net income should be close to R$170 million in 2025. This implies a P/E ratio below 11x, as well as the prospect of a high dividend yield.

Comparing this multiple with that of Moelis & Co, Lazard (LAZ), Evercore (EVR), PJT Partners (PJT), Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), and Houlihan Lokey, it is clear that there is a distortion in the valuation. Of these, the multiple that most closely approximates that of BR Partners is Lazard's forward P/E ratio of 22.5x, which is still twice as high.

With that, one could argue that BR Partners is a deep value case. It is greatly undervalued in relation to its peers, and just a re-rating caused by an improvement in the economic cycle and better institutional presence could unlock an upside of more than 50% without much optimism.

But in my view, on the spectrum between Deep Value and GARP, BR Partners is closer to GARP. We must remember that although the multiple is low, we are dealing with a Brazilian small cap. ROAE is high, but we must remember that Brazil's risk-free rate is 15%, so the discount rate is also quite high. Therefore, when compared to Brazilian players, the valuation gap is less apparent.

BTG Pactual trades at around 14x earnings, while Itaú Unibanco (ITUB), Brazil's most traditional retail bank, trades at around 10x.

Still, I believe that as BRBI builds better relationships with the US market, it is possible that its valuation will be better priced. Nu Holdings (NU) and MercadoLibre (MELI) are good examples of growth stories that, despite their significant exposure to Brazil, have managed to convince global investors to pay higher multiples to compensate for quality and growth.

In any case, I prefer to be conservative when trying to find the fair intrinsic value of BR Partners, and not count on a re-rating of its multiple to unlock all this upside in the short term.

For the assumptions, I considered revenue growth of 1% for next year, given the uncertainties, election year, and growth trend in recent quarters. After that, I considered a recovery in 2027 and 2028, and after that, a normalization to growth of only 5% per year, which is only slightly above Brazilian inflation and well below the full potential of the bank and the industry.

For the margin, I assumed a peak of 38% in 2028 (below what the company has already achieved in its best quarters), stabilizing at 35%.

Considering a terminal growth of 3% and discounting this net income flow by 15% (above Brazil's fixed-rate Treasury), we have an upside of 8%, even though we are being very conservative and using a high discount rate.

This proxy for a fair market cap of R$2.04 billion (~$385 million) implies that the IRR in BRL is 16%, an attractive level that shows how the company has the potential for compounding in the coming years.

Although it is difficult to have strong conviction in the Brazilian exchange rate, even if there is a slight devaluation of the BRL during this period, it already seems worth the risk. In fact, the Focus Report expects the exchange rate to remain stable until 2028, but given the current interest rates and uncertainties, I believe there will be at least a gradual devaluation in the long term due to the inflation differential.

The Bottom Line

In short, I believe that even with the uncertainties and challenges of the Brazilian scenario and exchange rate, BR Partners is a very compelling thesis in a growing market in Brazil.

Of course, the risks and volatility are higher than usual here, but a high IRR and considerable upside potential through re-rating and growth balance this out, making the risk-reward ratio attractive, especially when we consider that BRBI may be a potential GARP and already has a dividend yield close to double digits.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: Ur-Energy Turns A Corner Operationally, But Not Yet Financially
data.description:
After quite long of stagnation and losses Ur-Energy (NYSE:URG) finally showed real progress. In Q2 2025, the company made $10.4 million in revenue – this is first time the company is not only talking but showing that it can make money, but not yet the profits. The revenue mostly came from production and active deliveries, that is why it's first time. Lost Creek project, which was also stagnating, is not now only on paper, but showing results.

In a quarter they packed 112 thousand pounds of U₃O₈ and sold 105 thousand – this is +35% more than in Q1. So, this trend is showing that the company is nearing its annual anticipated plan – 440 thousand pounds. This is important not only for revenue, but for liquidity as well: this will let the company sell the unsold inventory (~400 thousand pounds).

Another signal – the costs are moving in the right direction. In 2024 one-unit cost was almost $50/lb, now in 2025 it is slightly reduced to $42.83/lb. If we compare it to the average sales price under the long-term contracts $61.56/lb, this gives a gross margin of around $18.73lb. This doesn't account for corporate overhead or capital costs just yet, but it shows that the company is progressing toward positive gross margin.

At the same time Shirley Basin project is being built according to plans – they have already built operations building, brought in main equipment and some of the drillings are starting as well. The main start is expected in early 2026 and from what I see the terms and budgets seem healthy.

It is important that the company still has $49.1 million cash, which gives a financial foundation to end 2025 without needing funding.

All in all, Ur-Energy after long waiting time showed that they can generate revenues. This is however not the case of turnaround just yet.

Risks

Even though Ur-Energy showed the first real sales, I still see some issues and instability risk. They are going from development to production phase, but financials have some holes in them.

Let’s start with losses which are still big. In the second quarter, they have experienced $21 million net loss, and in a half year more than $31 million. This means, that even when revenue started to show up on income statement, operational costs are still outrun it. At this moment, I don’t see them becoming profitable any time soon.

Another serious question is regarding the uranium derivative position, which in balance sheet shows almost $16 million loss. This is market to market obligation, which is directly related to uranium price. If the price of uranium goes down, the company would be obligated to cover the difference. This debt term is until November 2025, it seems like a short term, but this might become a pressure point.

Cash flows are showing problems as well. In first half they have burned through $9 million in operating cash flow and another $8.9 million was invested into projects and capital expenditure. Yes, in July the company had $49.1 million in cash, but this pillow might run out.

Another risk is margins. The contract sales price is $61.56/lb and in the second quarter production cost is $42.8/lb. So, as I mentioned, this leaves with a gross margin of about 18.73/lb, healthy indeed, but not good enough once capital costs are included. If the price of uranium goes down below $60 in spot market, the company would either experience negative gross margin. This could leave the company in unhedged production as well.

Main risk, which I don’t like at all is capital attraction. Even if they have cash today, without additional revenues or actual margin improvement, the future might bring us to new share issuance or debt. This means that shareholder value would go down and the interest rates would give additional pressure. But as I said, this is speculation, that this might happen, currently I don’t see any signs of it coming.

Catalysts

I have said many negative things, let’s move towards the positives. In short-term I see some very good catalysts that would change the situation.

First – production at Lost Creek project. Q2 as I mentioned packed 112 thousand pounds of uranium, which was 35% more than in Q1. The yearly plan is nearing the contracted 400 thousand. If they succeed in this and let’s say “maybe” go above, this excess could be sold in spot market which would mean additional revenue without big costs.

Second – Shirley Basin. The project is moving according to plan. As I said they have built operational building and so on. The start is planned in early 2026, and management expect the unit cost to be at $24.4/lb, which would be even more effective than Lost Creek, so with this project I expect margins to improve significantly.

Third is macroeconomics. US has issued new Nuclear Fuel Security Act with $2.7 billion budget for local uranium producers. At the same time, they have banned LEU import from Russia. Geopolitical tensions in Kazakhstan and Nigeria making tensions on supply as well. It means that US producers (Ur-Energy as well) will have strong demand from government and from private buyers.

The contracts are good as well, the company has eight long term contracts (for 6 million pounds). If deliveries are going to be timely without any issues, this would guarantee stable revenues and stronger reputation as trustworthy supplier.

Summary

Ur-Energy after long wait started showing real results – the sales happened, Lost Creek production is growing, Shirley Basin project is moving according to plan. This is first signal that they are going from promises to real operations phase. But financial side is not yet in order: the losses are still significant, and the derivative debt is making some pressure (and to be honest this is the main red flag I am afraid of).

Positive thing is that US macro environment is becoming more and more suitable for local uranium producers: due to government support, due geopolitical risks in the world. Ur-Energy here can use their situation.

All in all, while losses are not easing and margins stay stagnated, this stock is still risky. I believe, this is the story about potential, rather than actual stable profit. Because of that, I am still maintaining HOLD, I will be waiting for few more quarters to see if production becomes financial stability.
